speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This interview is with Tom Platt, Dave Hickman, and Kenneth Platt. Topics discussed are the Platt cattle ranching operations centered on the Salmon River rangeland with support from their farm, Genesee, the settlement and strategic location of early Genesee and wheat raising in the area, and the livery business and annual horse show and rodeo in the town.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, if you give us some idea what you're looking for or what you're thinking about, Kenny said something about the town. I think the town, the early day memories that come to your mind about what it was like growing up around there, what it was like being on the farm, things that you remember that are interesting to you. I think your early recollections of the town would be a good place to start. I was born and raised right in Genesee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tom came there when?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I was born there, but of course we went back to Salmon River and stayed there. I never got back to stay there until 1909 when I started school, fall of 1909. And I was there for three, during the school term, for three years and then wasn't back until I started high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your family had a place on Salmon too?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they ranching there?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Cattle.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: But they lived there all the time. So he knows a great deal more about it than I do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the town like there when you were just a kid?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh, every business house was filled up. Every place along the line had something in it. My dad ran the livery stable there and that was just about the east end of town as far as any business was concerned. But across from us was Creamery. Up the line a little ways was a hardware store, furniture store, and then just keep right on going through town. Every place was filled up. Everything was lively. Well, there were two or three blacksmith shops later. Of course, some of those turned, a couple of them turned into garages. But at that time, why they were blacksmith shops were chewing horses and fixing farm equipment.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't have any idea how many places there were that were actually places of business, but I can remember when there were a couple of grocery stores and two or three, two merchandise stores. There were mercantile stores where they handled dry goods and this type of thing. And my recollection of the thing I like to remember about it is that where we lived down on the flat was right in the area where they had the old ball field one time. And the city ball field was right close there, but it was also the place where the Indians came when the Indians moved into town or came in for a celebration or they came down and stayed right down on that flat close to us. And they would go back and forth, of course, from town. And it seemed like the recollection that I have mostly of Genesis is the old horseshoes. Three days of horse show every spring and usually two days of rain. So they were always assured of a good crop. They had lots of big horses, lots of draft horses, and lots of, oh, not lots, but quite a few head of Herefords and big cattle of some kind there that they were raising. And lots of big old perch and horse perch and belgian shire horses. And of course, they're delivery stable. There was always two or three stud horses that were standing there during the season to take care of the mares. But I can remember those, especially those horse shows, because they were always assured of at least two days of rain out of the three. It was in June. Well, it sounds like it was a three -day affair and then it was quite a big celebration for the town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, yes. It was three days of the horse show and rodeo. It was a horse show and rodeo combined. And I can remember one time of old sundown Jackson, this first Indian that was world champion rider, being there at the fair. And he gave me the job of, I was just a kid around the livery stable, but he gave me the job of exercising his horse for him. And I took the horse out for a little ride in the morning. Then that afternoon, he would take the horse and ear him down and put a hackamore on him and throw the saddle on him. And that thing would just buck and ball. You'd think he was an outlaw. But he was just as tame as could be if he wanted him. Then after it was all over, he'd put the saddle back on him and away they'd go again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, so what, do you remember the horse shows too?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I'd like you guys would describe some more about what it was like, what they did there, and just what the celebration was.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It sounds like they showed a lot of horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they local stock mostly?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh yes, I think most of it was local stock. I think most of the farmers had good stock. And then they were, and this stock was bred to good stallions that were locally, kept around there locally. As I remembered, it was simply a showing of horses, not so much saddle horses, although there were some saddle horses, but mostly draft horses, with a few cattle in the picture too. And the prizes, I don't have any idea now what the prizes were that were offered, but I suppose they weren't very much in cash now compared to what they would have now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was this a Genesee specialty?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh yes. It wasn't something that they did all over the place. No, no, no, it was one year thing, and as far as I know, a Genesee horse show was Genesee's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I doubt if anybody further away than Colton ever got in there, did they?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't suppose. Not many people would get to it anyway.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Didn't have any trucks in those days to put these things around.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: A lot of folks come in from the country for those days and staying town. Oh, they had real big crowds, as I remember. Yeah, the Indians would come up from the reservation and stay down there in the flats, and of course they took part in the rodeo and also in the fair.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They were quite a piece of the parade too. There was quite a parade, all these horses and stock and cowboys and engines and little of everything mixed up there.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: That sounds a little different than Community Day now that they have. Oh yes, Community Day is just something for people to come back to and get acquainted again. This was strictly a stock show. They were showing horses, that's all there was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the other livestock? Was that part of it then?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I remember some cattle, but I don't remember very many cattle. I think it was mostly horses.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Plus, plus paraded a few Herefords and normies paraded a few short horns. That was about it. I don't remember any dairy cattle that were being in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The pigs? What about the horses that were shown mostly? Were there certain guys that had certain special kind of horses that were the kind that they used? Were there certain guys that had Persians mainly and other guys that had other kinds?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I think this is right. Some farmers just prided themselves on their livestock that they had. This was mostly working stock, that is they worked them on the ranch. Of course, they had some mares that they kept for breeding stock. Some of the farmers, of course, just used mixtures of Cayuse and Morgan and Belgium or anything they could get a hold of. These things that were shown on the horse show day were good stock. Probably all had ribbons on them.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I would imagine that all the strategies that came up that street were registered. Oh, yes. I'm sure they were. They were real good for all the animals.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I suppose you'd say even a good deal more proud of those animals that they worked those days than the farming out here today. As it was D6 or H or all we happens to have.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Some of those folks were pretty proud of their livestock and the ancestry and the breeding and so forth that they had mixed up in them.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: And they knew about it too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there breeds of horses that were agreed on as being the best for work horses? Were there any outstanding?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I would think you're running the same thing that you do with beef cattle or dairy cattle now, the individual. A man who likes a certain individual type of animal were treated better and he would get along better with it than a natural lady likes it better. One man may like a shire, another man a perch in any way.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Perchins, Belgians, Shires, Morgans. Any number of them. Of course the Morgan was more or less of a coach horse that was introduced in order to make better driving ponies and lighter horses. But as Tom says, the Shires and the Belgium, this type of thing were the ones that perchins were the ones that they really liked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they race at all in the horse show? Were there races?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't remember anything except maybe there was a saddle horse race. If they were, it was in conjunction with the rodeo. I don't remember. The first time I said something about the old flat down where we lived, when these horse shows were on, they were on a flat then closer to town or on the other side of the town. That's the same place now that they have the ball field today. This is where they used to have their rodeos.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the rodeo anything like it is today with the roping?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Yeah.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It was about the same thing except they didn't saddle in the chutes. They saddled out on the ground.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Aired them down. A couple of guys snubbed them up and went ahead.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They had pulled up and went back. Well, they didn't have that rodeo that I remember. They rode some stairs. They had wild horses.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: You know, rocks and that stuff.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't remember them turning loose wild horses to be roped out there on the ground. No, I don't either.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: But then they had lots of bad things going on all the time. But it wasn't a three -ring circus like they have now with all the things that were going on. They attracted some good people. For instance, this Jackson Sundown, or Sundown Jackson was the world's champion at Pendleton. It was a good enough show. He was attracted anyway. Now whether it was the prizes or whether it was just close to home, I don't know which. But he was there and riding.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: There were other good riders there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pruitt?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Yeah, Pruitt was up there. A couple of Pruitt's. White. Bills and Weeks. I don't know who all.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Boy, if you didn't tell him, I forgot his name. He was a pretty good guy. He never looked good.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: These are all guys that were in the rodeo.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say this was the biggest event of the year for the town?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh yes, there's no question about it because it was the only real event that they had at that time. As Tom says, the Indians made up a good share of the show because they were in the parade and taken part. Then they took part in the rodeo, too, some of them, the ones that wanted to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you describe what you remember about your father's delivery, the blacksmith in operation there? What he did?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, he had a livery stable, but not blacksmith. There were at least two blacksmiths in town at the time, but he didn't have one. The earliest memory I have of it was probably after it was going downhill a little bit because I only remember him having about 12 or 14 horses, that is, six or eight teams, seven teams. They had teams that were hired out for people to drive and then other teams that they furnished drivers with. At one time, this was in the early days, Genesee was the end of the railroad long before the railroad got into Moscow. So anyone wanting to go to Lewiston came to Genesee and then worked the town there, the dry goods part of it, the dry goods salesmen, and then they took a hack and went from there on down to Lewiston. This was a regular run every so often for the livery stable to take two or three drummers down to Lewiston with their suitcases and sample bags and so forth. And it was only about 20 miles, so this wasn't so awfully far to travel. Then later, oh, about 1914, 1915, somewhere along there, then there was a couple of automobiles.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: 1912, Beli and 1913 Rio, and ran a taxi service then for the next three or four years. And I'm not just sure which year it was or where it comes in, but oh, about 1917, 1918, somewhere along about wartime, then the dad went to work for Tom and Kenny's dad for the Pilat brothers. And then he farmed for them for a couple of years and then went out on his own later and bought a farming outfit. But that was the end of the livery stable anyway, about the first of the World War.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it had to do with the changing of automobiles that made him decide to work those business?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I'm sure this was part of it. Of course, at that time, in the early days, there were no roads. There still weren't any roads in 1915, 1916. There were still all dirt roads except that one stretch of road east of Genesee, which had been put in, oh, somewhere around 1910, probably. They had put in a mile or a mile or a little over a rock road there. And this was the only rock road in the country. The rest of it was all dirt. So automobiles weren't very proper in the wintertime for quite some time, but then eventually, why they would be able to get gravel roads in the country.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Would you be happy to know that a day before they rocked the streets in Genesee, before they rocked that road? Which came from?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I always thought, I don't know, I always thought that the road out there by the old east end warehouse was one of the first ones put in. It was put in out of that quarry at the ranch. I'm sure it was the first road outside of town, but I was just wondering whether they rocked Main Street there before they rocked that or something like that. I don't know. Your dad, Kenny, talks about that in his book.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Do you remember what the reference was?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I remember reading part of it. One of the references he said about it, they built it out as a heavy rock, just very little crushing, if any, to it anyway. The farmers, when they came in, they would drive on the side of the road, and instead of driving on top of the rock, they stayed off of it until it finally got enough dirt mixed in it, but the rock didn't stick up quite so bad. I don't know whether he gives a date in there when this was built or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let me just ask you, describing how he used the teams for taking the salesman, what other ways did he rent his team? Did he freight very much? Was that part of the livery business?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He just always rented teams?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Just rented teams or fed horses, of course, coming in would be stable over day or night or for a meal or two or whatever part that people wanted to study. This way, there was always some animals in there being fed and to keep a few stallions, this type of thing, and were always kept there year -round. But no, there was no freight, no actual hauling out of there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did farmers rent teams from him to do their farm work, or was that usually taken care of by their own?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They owned their own horses, and mostly the renting of the teams was to people from town or drummers traveling through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any recollection or idea of how many horses you could disable there at a time?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: No, I'd have to make an awful guess. I suppose in the neighborhood of 45, 50 head there, they could have been put up inside the barn without using the corral on the outside. There was quite a corral there too with mangers around the outside, so it could have been, I would imagine, he could have put up 100 head horses there without stretching things too far.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he take over that setup from the previous operator?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: He bought Sheldon out.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Bob mentioned housing a company of cavalry there.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I always assumed it was that stable. It was before my time then, because that had to have been, well, I don't know what that had to have been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to say that probably was when the fort was still there, or was this...?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It was when he was a boy.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It had been a little while before my time. When he was a boy, it was old town anyway. I mean, Genesee, we know the name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? That was the older town before?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: There's two Genesee's. He means that Genesee originally was on the corner out beyond the East End warehouse, if you're quite aware of it, Genesee at all. It went around that corner and over toward the cemetery. It was on both sides of the road. Then when the railroad built in there, somebody tried to hold them up anyway. Instead of coming on clear into what was the town at that time, they stopped where Genesee is now and said, build your town over here. So this is what happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any? How much of that town had to be moved then? Was there much there at the time the railroad came in?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't think they moved very much. They just built a new town.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I think so. There was almost a stage stop and a couple of stores out there, Levi's. But no, I think it mostly just went to Botts out there. They just left it. I don't think they ever moved very much of it in the town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there still much of the old traces there when you were east of the old town?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Now it's all far. They must have been close to a dozen buildings out there when we were just here. Now it's all farmland.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They're all gone now.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: The story that I read about that, Valmer said it was Jake Rosenstein that was coming up there. The real world. That was what Valmer said. Well, this could be.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: There's one name that I keep kicking around and I don't know where he fits in, but this Levi had a store one time there in the early days. Of course, Rosenstein had a store in my town. Then after him, his son, Max Rosenstein, ran the store there until it finally went out of business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what Rosenstein's store was like?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: That was the biggest dry goods store here in Genesee. Oh, yes. It was quite a store. It had a little of everything in it. They were good people to run the store. They wouldn't let you get out without buying something. If you came in there to buy something, you were just about going to buy it. The only thing I remember much about the Rosenstein's was there were two girls, and as far as I know, they're still alive.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: One of them died, I think, about 600 months ago in Los Angeles. No, wait a minute now. I'm thinking of this girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was Jewish, right? Did he take a very after part in the community life?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I just don't remember.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they have in their book about Mox and his Chinese clothesline? Mox and his what?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: The Chinaman's clothesline. He had a Chinese laundry there. Mox cut the clothesline. He cut the clothesline.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why the clothesline?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: The clothesline got caught at it.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't remember if they had any active part in the affairs of the town at all, or even who was the mayor or the council or anything else in those days. Well, you guys and kids, you didn't care about that stuff, no. Well, my dad, though, for a while after he quit the stable, was a street man and policeman and water superintendent and everything. I suppose the job probably that the present time takes about two or three people to do the same thing. But in those days, that's all there was to it. One of the things I remember a long time back is a fellow named Morgan, who was the head of the telephone company in there. And he lived way up on the North Hill, and he would drive in with this old car, he is, and I don't even remember what make it was, a two or three cylinder car, two cylinder maybe. And he'd get just about 50 yards up this first hill if it was the least bit slick. Well, that's as far as he ever went, and then he'd go home and get a pair of mules and take the car on home.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Now, I don't know why, at that time he always went up that hill there by the old Herman furniture store. But now there's a pretty good road up the draw where he could have gone, or it looks like he could have gone, but maybe in those days it was, it would have bogged him down too. Maybe Ben Sowette, he couldn't even get through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember when that telephone came into Genesee? I don't really mean to hear, but were you kids at the time? Did telephone service came? Was it there as long as you can remember?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It was there as long as I can remember.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, Dad had his phone there in the barn, and so it had to have been in there. Oh, and after 1910, 1912, somewhere along the mare that they put it in. And farmers had it, I guess, in the country, although I can't remember anything about the service or anything like that. Maybe that's like the old, they tell about the old phones that were hooked up on the barbed wire fences, because they had lots of barbed wire fences.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: See, Genesee was only three miles, actually, from the first Indian reservation, the north boundary of the reservation. Although it's practically all owned now by white men. There's no Indian land left at all that I know of down there, although it probably is so. But at that time, they traveled back and forth to Genesee right along, do some of their trading.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: And so he has all that stuff that gambles used to have in there, and James' head, and sweet knees, and stamp maker.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you guys remember about particular Indians that you knew when you were young?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh, I can remember going with that Uncle of mine, Eli, when it was falling. One old Indian up there that he was quite good friends with, and that was Pio Patalic. Outside of Sundown Jackson or some of those fellows, I really can't remember any names.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know any of them?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh, yes. There were a number of Indian kids in school there when I went to high school.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: This one family that moved in each fall to send those kids to school lived on their own ranch right there. And one boy that stayed with them, one winter and one schooler, it was Dave Miles, who was a teacher and a Presbyterian minister now.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: And the human real well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was it we went to see very funny?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: That's falling.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh, yeah, Dan Arthur.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I remember the Arthurs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were young, there were still a fair number of Indians that had land around Tennessee, right?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh, yes. Lots of them down on the reservation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any family that was older, Indian family?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They had to farm their land about as long as they had Indian.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Lawrence Pouts place today. When I worked for Neil Swing, that 80 there belonged to an Indian named Cook. He used to farm it. He'd come up and work on a fishing machine there. Apparently he sold it up to Lawrence or something.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, they're still scattered pieces of Indian land and the clients make Indian land in that area. Of course, you have to remember that when we were kids, there was someone living on every 80 acres or every 160 at least. Now there's someone living on every half section probably. Part of that maybe. Or maybe even not that much. Average size farm is over 800 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this land originally part of the reservation before? What largely happened was that Indians sold the land?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: No. The land right around Tennessee, the land that we still have there, the Hickman family still have, whether this was Platt Brothers at one time or was Platt. It was homesteaded by a man named Hanson. Then it was bought by Platt, our grandfather. Then this is the land that we still have there. You'd have to go about two miles or two and a half miles south before you get into the reservation. But the land right around Tennessee was all homesteaded.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, the story is that our folks came there in, I believe, 79. 81.

speaker: Sam Schrager
William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, that's when Mother was born, was in 81. But at that time the land around Tennessee was all taken up, so they had to buy land.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They could have come farther up towards Moscow Mountain here and got land, but around Tennessee it was all taken up at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have an idea of why you picked that place, why we can't see what you wanted?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, the story was that they came to Uniontown and stayed with a sister, and that is Grandad's sister, and stayed over winter with them. They were about five or six miles south of Uniontown. Apparently they had in mind coming to this Genesee Valley all the time because then the next year they moved on up into Genesee Valley.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: The way I understand it, they lived out on the old Perkins place for some time. And then finally bought this place from Hanson, but we still have. But my dad didn't come there until 89, and he came from North Carolina and just bought him a ticket to the end of the railroad, and that was the end of the railroad. So that's how he landed there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how he started in 89? Did he go there?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: He started working on, he worked on orchards down on the Snake River and worked on ranch there for a year or two before we finally bought him this livery stable. But he worked there a little while anyway on some of these farms. Well, when Mr. Black came in 81, the land was old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how developed it was, though? Was it very much under the plow already? Was it a bunch of it producing crop?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I would doubt that. They had no great place to send a crop, no way to get rid of it.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They had to take it to all of them. They had to feed it.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: The grain they had to feed the pigs and drive the pigs somewhere. They had to feed the cattle or horses or something of that sort. And there's another factor in there. There's been a big climate change because it was years before they could raise wheat on the Genesee class.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: 1917. First war to get them started.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Before that, at the time we were talking about, in the 1880s, those flats were Timothy and Clover. Of course there was a big demand for hay then. All this work was horses, all transportation was horses. Unless it was even slower with oxen.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: So the whole climate has changed. We have some pictures around someplace, but one of them I think is with your dad. It's taken in front of Paula's store and you can just see his head over the snow banks where they shoveled the snow off.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: That was 1912 or 1913 when they tunneled through in order to get back and forth from one side to the street to the other.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't know. There's been a tremendous change. So stock was much more important in the early days than it was after the war. And it continued to be important for a long time because they drove stock from over around Grangeville. Even drove hogs from over in there, Cottonwood and Grangeville. The Genesee was the end of the railroad. That's the only place they could go. That's what made Genesee for a long time.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: During the war they came in there with the horses for the French cavalry before we got mixed up in the war. And they brought those, Salmon River and Grangeville and over in that country. Brought them across the Indian Reservation and probably picked up a pony or two as they came along. Anyway, they sold them out then. And the idea there was that they had to ride them through the gate in order to show that they were broken. Some of them hadn't even had the saddle on them before. But then they rode them through the gate anyway.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I can remember that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who grew them? Who raised the horses?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Salmon River. I'm not even sure where the horses came from in the first place. But they came off of the Salmon River breaks down there someplace. Somebody was in the horse business.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Just about everybody over there had a few horses.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It's funny because now you're thinking of this so completely farming country. You don't think of it as much as being horse country. You know that is so much of its stock country. But if every farm land or every piece of land say 160 acres had to have, well we had eight or ten horses there when we farmed that 160 there. 170 acres. And if every piece of land had that many horses, there was an enormous amount of that land that was going right back into feed and grain to feed those horses. Well, the captain and Driscoll out there were their big outfit they had and they had a bunch of mules.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Thirty -forty mules around.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't know how many mules they would have to farm a layout like they had, but it was a good many of them. They took seven horses usually on a gangplow, two bottom plow. Now they start out with a eight or six or eight bottom plow buying one of these tractors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the marketing problems? Could you remember a bunch about what that was like, what they used to say it was like in the early days?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, when you got to the point where you had transportation and could sell grain and stuff like that for cash, it was the same as it is today as far as I know. And of course the big packing companies were not long in establishing packing houses. Spokane, Seattle, and Coeur d'Alene.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: You can remember Tom Noland when they had hay warehouse there at Genesee. They had apple packing house there. One time I don't remember it necessarily, but they must have had prune dryers because there were prune orchard scattered all over that area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the Genesee slaughterhouse slaughter for out shipments or just local that was still operating?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Yeah, in my time old western sea and raiders and so forth, they were just doing their own butchering or butchering for people, local people. I don't know that they were butchering for anybody out of town at all. Well, I was thinking more about the time back before 1900 even. I mean those really early years when you were saying there wasn't much to market. I would imagine that there would be a lot of difficulty for it. Well, Tom mentioned driving pigs now. I can't imagine driving pigs, but this was one of Millard's dad's stories that he used to tell how they used to raise pigs out here in this landfill country where they lived, the Armstrong's. And then when they got them ready for market, they'd drive them to Colfax across the country. Well, I can't imagine driving pigs, but that's...

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It don't seem possible, but I guess they did it. I don't know my dad. But the first time he saw old birds, they were coming on to pray with the drive of pigs, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like getting down to Lewiston from Tennessee in the early days? What was it like to get down? Was that grade pretty tough?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, all of the grades had been improved time after time, but the old central grade, there was a grade that came up through by Archibalds and came off that central grade. And then the old Uniontown grade, they've been there for, well, I guess ever since there have been people up on these hills because they... Just here a while back, they made this Collins place, a historical site, the house and the granary, and that was right on the old stage road. And then, of course, there was another stage road that came out of Lewiston and came up through Genesee when Genesee was down at the old tower and went right on through past the fort and came up through Moscow and Farmington. I believe Farmington was the end of the route. And then they turned around there and went on back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you were trading, would you be more likely to trade Lewiston or Moscow from Genesee?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, I don't know. I would suspect that at that time you'd have to stay in Genesee because I think they had the transportation and the goods in the stores.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I was thinking of the bigger, if you had to go to a big town, which was a town that Genesee would feel closer to as far as that sort of purchasing.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't know.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I would imagine Lewiston, because Lewiston in those days was what they called a seaport. They were bringing boats in there in the early days. In fact, they tell the story that when they were building later, building that railroad up the river, the Orofino up in there, that one railroad ran a boat with a high mast up through there and made it put in drawbridges all the way to Kooskia while the other railroad was building in there. So that whole area is drawbridges. But they knocked the bottom out of the boat every time they went up and back. That didn't matter.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't know. Like Tom says, they were pretty well self -supporting, but I've heard them old -timers tell, and Mildred's dad was one of them, about going to Palouse to get their flower ground because there was a flower mill at Palouse. Of course, I imagine there was a flower mill in, maybe in Colfax or maybe Lewiston and some of these other towns all this time, too.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: So they'd have to go that far to...

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They'd go over one day and get their quick ground and then lay over a day and then bring it home the next day. Or just trade it for wheat. I don't know which they would do. That's pretty interesting because I know that, of course, people in the North End of the county went to Palouse, but I had no idea that it's... The wall was so big, they could be able to move all the way down to Gen C.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you guys know your grandfather, Flat? Was he still alive?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about him?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I even remember great -grandfather Harris, but mostly what I can remember about him is the old corn cake that he used to smoke in that pipe that he has.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I remember him better than Grandfather Flat. He was in that little house back at the church there when I lived with Grandfather's Day in school there.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: He came over.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, Grandpa Flat apparently was quite a public -minded citizen because he was mixed up in quite a few things of helping to promote the community.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: But this great -grandfather, we talk about Harris, he was an old Methodist minister and retired at the time Tom and I knew him. But he married my dad and mother in 1998. And then he was retired, of course, when I knew him. But he used to come down every Sunday, or almost every Sunday, and have dinner with us down at the house and sit on the back porch and watch the ball game and smoke his corn cob pipe and give the folks the fits for playing ball on Sunday. But he didn't miss a day anyway to come down.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: He'd been in Genesee then as a minister in the real early days. He was a minister at Leland when he married dad and mother. He came from England. But no, I don't know that he ever was a minister in Genesee.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, he didn't come even until after Grandpa and Grandma were settled there.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: No, that's right. He wasn't one of the real early ones. He came later anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about the farm when you were a kid growing up there? Did your father have much going by then?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: A pretty big operation.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, pretty special, yes. The most part I remember about his work.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: No, he had one big ranch in there.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They farmed that ranch there in town, 170 acres or so. Then they rented a little more around there. Then they had 700 acres out on that fault place, plus the canyon, which was another 700 acres or so. That all was farmed with horses.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Besides Tom's folks there, or Tom and Kenny's folks both, had at least 200 heads of purebred Herefords that were kept there on the place in town. I say they were kept there later when they got to the fault place while they were running the canyons too, some of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know about when they started bringing in the purebred stock?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I would think it was the spring of 1915.

speaker: Sam Schrager
William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It could be. It was just before the start of the war, I imagine.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I suspect that they brought the first full Hereford cattle to Idaho. I don't know of any others or even folks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you know about how he brought them in, where he got them from and how he got them?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: He got them in Iowa, Iowa and Nebraska. He just went back there and picked out what he wanted and bought them. I guess I should have put it another way. He picked out what he could for and shipped them out here.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Later some of them came from Kansas City when they went to that, took a bunch of stock back to that show back there. They came back with a bigger carload that they took back in the first place.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, he went back that other time and was getting some money and he called for blows. He couldn't begin to supply it. They went back there and bought a carload of the young bulls. They shipped out here and brought Archie Kane along with them.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Archie Kane was the herdsman for some time.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: By that time they were putting out a show her. They didn't think that hurt me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what made your father decide to go from his earlier operation into a purebred?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: No, I don't really. Maybe he just wanted into it.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I guess it probably seemed like a good investment at the time. I guess probably it was. Things broke along.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They always told me, both John and Bill, two brothers, that they owed about $125 ,000 to an outfit that was investing in livestock. It was a livestock organization that loaned money. They had offered to pay it up time and again, but this outfit was never interested in being paid up. It was just a good investment for them to leave it there and take the interest on it. Well then, when things began to get tough and they really got out to the place where the boys were getting pinched a bit, and then this outfit stepped in and said, well, we have to have our money. And what they did, well, that was practically the end of it.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It's a Portland loan.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I can remember purebred Herefords being sold for $32, heifers being sold for $32 a head. Right at the end of the Depression or right at the start of the Depression. Of course, they certainly couldn't come out on anything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, from the beginning of the purebred operation, about how many did he start with, you know?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: From what size it was.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Do you remember how many extensions there was on each side of the old barn there?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: No, I don't. I would suppose 20 on each side probably.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Was there that many?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I know in 19... yeah, the winter in 17. After I got out of the hospital with the typhoid, they went up there and helped me feed them and they put the cows all in. I can remember there was 12 in that old barn that sets with a horse barn, where they built the new horse barn. And we put the rest of them in the stanchions on the two sides of the old barn and we had everything covered. Now that was the extent of the cow herd.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: 45, 50 head at the most, that probably was.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: So I would say that he brought a car load and those were cattle that you tied up in the car. So probably he started with around 25 head cows and heifers.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: But the winter, one winter there when we worked at the... Dad and I hauled straw, and that's probably the only thing we did that winter, was break... ...trail out of there to haul straw with four horses for bedding. And I'm sure that at that time they had about 200 head of purebreds. So that kept building up anyway, where they had quite a few head.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember how many years later that would have been?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, that wouldn't have been...

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It was early 20s.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, I wasn't even going to say it was that far along. Maybe it was 19. But in 1921 or 22, Dad was farming the Follett Place. He was out there. So it was before that time. So it might have been just about in 1920.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he find much demand for the stock once he started going with it?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, it's the thing that goes up and down. When cattle are high, there's a big demand for good books because people are stocking up for cattle. When they go down, people begin to sell off, then your market begins to drop on them too. So it's an up and down, roller coaster sort of a thing, really. I don't know how you get away from that. It seems to just go through all the stock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that his bringing in the purebred burfards had a much of an effect on the stock growing in the area?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Do you think people started getting a lot more interested in this type of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: a real good breed of animal?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, there is a stockman, if he's any good, he's always looking to better himself. He knows that when he betters his cattle, he betters himself. So yes, I think that it could be illegal.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: This would be like when you said the demand for bulls. That would be that kind of thing.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Of course, you have to remember, too, that these two fellas had...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: oh, what?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: 1100 head of range stock on the Salinger. They took some bulls for that to keep that outfit going. They owned that much, you see, besides a couple bands of sheep. Actually, the demand for purebred bulls was not in the Genesee community. It was in the outlying range areas where range cattle were used. As far as I know, my father and uncle were the only purebred producers in the Genesee area. They didn't sell, meaning to other farmers in that area where sales were farther away.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: A few out on the breaks, people that had pasture, like Clearwater Ranch, the Swings, and some of those people that had a lot of canyon pasture out there, gave them the ballpoint.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I think you could say that the whole development of the Genesee related to other areas pretty much throughout its history. When our grandparents came there, as Dave said, the land was already taken up. They had to buy land. They couldn't homestead. When my father got old enough to start doing stuff on his own, there was not an opportunity to go into farming in the Genesee area without buying land or renting.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Our grandfather sent him off to the town.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Because this was before the time of automobiles, there was quite a bit of refinement in the use of horses. As Dave mentioned, his dad ran a livery stable, but he didn't do freight hauls. In town, the local horn was done by people in the dream business. It was a dream. It was going farther. It was freeing. So it was really special. There was a lot of specialization in these important things. In those days, every grocery store made deliveries around town. They would have their own cart or their own rig, or else they rented it from a dray outfit to do their delivering for them. This was a big business to haul everything around to the people that they bought. Maybe just a little bit of groceries, but then whatever it was, you had to haul it. You had to deliver it in those days.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Even in our time, a couple of families were living on that right in Genesee. There was some guilt. Two dray lines.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, everybody didn't have a pickup truck. Everybody didn't have one ton or anything of the kind. So if they wanted coal or anything else, somebody had to haul it to them.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It's the plain fact that they couldn't get enough decent wolves. We had some pretty poor wolves in there. They were registered wolves and all that. There just wasn't enough good ones around, and they wanted better ones when they couldn't get them.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: To go back a little farther than your questioning went, Sam, our grandfather went to a Herbert Hereford Boon sale at Hamilton, I think it was. The Grand. It was the Grand. That was in 1996.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I believe that's right.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They bought, I think, three bulls and put them in the Ranger's family. To begin with, they were the laughingstock of the range community. They were much smaller, looked smaller than the big, long -legged cattle. But at the end of the second year, when they began to have saleable steers, they found out that they would be way bigger than the other cattle a year older. And this kind of triggered the demand then for Herbert Hereford. They introduced Herbert to the range cattle industry in the Sam River country, and this germinated the demand for them. So this was one of the many factors that kind of worked together to get my dad and his brother into the Herbert cattle business 20 years later.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: They were known as having introduced these bulls. They had the reputation of having the best range cattle in the area where they operated. It was a chore to get enough good bulls, so it was a logical compliment to their range staff to start producing some good germinated cattle of their own. They supplied part of their own demand, all of their own demand, part of some other people's demand. And it made the logical combination of enterprises. They had this family farm, that they had to take over responsibility for operating because their grandfather had died in 1912. And I guess they rented the place out for a few years, and they didn't crowd rent the farm place. I don't think crowd ever did, but he farmed it anyway.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Stucker had it for a year or two, and Post had it for a couple of years. I don't know if there was anybody else.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Do you know of anybody else's name?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, if they got back onto it in 1915, that would be about the limit, unless someone read it and used it, part of their grandfather is still alive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know much about how the San River operation was set up? What kind of an area they had and the seasonal work situation down there?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, tell them we'll remember that.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, it was a typical range cattle out, but as far as this area is concerned, it's rough country, very rough. Cheekgrass, bunch of grass, not very much brush. Pretty well watered. The summer down the mountain. It would be the same as if you come up from Lewis and then summer here and meadows up here. Same thing, I had meadows there, timbers, summer there. Raised enough hay on the river to winter the cattle by irrigating it off.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Then of course it was grass -baked, they didn't feed anything at that time.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Usually sold to buyers from old, some of the packers in Spokane or Seattle or somewhere like that. Standing in pride.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Cattle were ordinarily sold right on the range for the buyers in Cane. Quite often.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: But those buyers like John Bear, for instance, he represented the Spokane Opera. He was buying with their checkbook.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: He was wearing on the same river was the operation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know where Winchester is?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It's been in Winchester. Well, about 20 miles straight south. It's due south.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: In general it was on both sides of the river from there to the mountain river. Interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That area was heavily used by a number of ranchers in those days?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Yeah, there was lots of homesteaders in there. They were very late surveying that either around 19, 9 or 10 that they were surveyed out. And afterwards, after they became surveyed, the homesteaders just walked in there and took it off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your father's place purchased or was it homestead?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It was his homestead.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, there was some irrigated land later on this Deer Creek place, wasn't there?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: There was, I don't know, they used to call it 20 acres. I question the 20. But that's what they called it. And it was very productive. It had never been really crapped to see it was virgin land. He put water on alfalfa and that kind of soil and it would do pretty well.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't know. He bought West's relinquishment on that. But it had never been surveyed at the time. So actually he's the original entry homie.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Couldn't say he bought West's relinquishment. It was West's squatters' right and West bought his from another fellow.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: No one knew where they were, what they were taken up or where it was.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It's not according to the survey or anything like that. He just had a place there and if he was there in possession, nobody bothered you. At that particular time there was lots of land. But a few years later it was gone. But of course these homesteaders led this such steep country that they had no way on 160 to make a living. Most spent the winter in what part of the spring they couldn't find a job on the place. They planted the garden and built a house and managed to prove up and sold it. If they couldn't sell it to one of the stockmen, they mortgaged it to the bank and let the bank take it over.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: What about the old Deer Creek Mine there? Was it early days or wasn't it?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, the lower, the Deer Creek Mine was fairly early days. The Horseman Mine on top of the hill which I used.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: That's the one I was talking about. I thought maybe it was.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: But that came along just before the first World War. But the other one was around 1900 on down below where they had the big tunnel and where they put in the mill down there. I don't know, that was kind of a typical Vince If somebody wanted to take a chance on how long those prices would set up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could we talk? Sure, would you have more?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: That's funny.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work out there much when you were a kid out of place?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh yes, all the time.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I don't know how old he was when he used to ride at these rodeos and thought he was making pretty good money getting $5 a head for riding ponies out of the chute.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, I'd like to know something a little bit about that when you were a kid working out in a place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of work you did?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh, I did about everything. This little hagie fed a few cows with it. Did about everything you do on Sad Horse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you starting to do some pretty man -sized work when you were just a kid then?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, I don't know. I used to go up to my dad to the mountain and have to move the cattle out and he'd go off and leave me. To run the place when I was 9, 10 years old. So I guess I was doing what a man does.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh boy. Oh, that was pretty good.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Yeah. Tie it together. You'd have to put the salmon over those Genesee operations together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did they fit together?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, after the purebred cattle operation was set up, of course they were a natural complement to the extent that the Genesee ranch produced the bird bulls for the range operation.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: We never used that Genesee operation to provide feed for the range.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: That was an entirely different type of wintering. You were not entirely truthful there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they send some grain to the cattle from there?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: No. But in the beginning the cattle were brought, beef cattle were brought from Sandler and fed to Genesee. Oh, I didn't know about that.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just before they were sold?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It was sold as fed cattle rather than grass cattle.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: This is in the beginning now when my grandfather was running the operation and my dad was just a kid to help me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was that some use that was made of hay that Pop used to bale then when he was running the bailing outfit?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I suspect he didn't bale very much plant hay. I think he was in that for a few side dollars. That's what I thought. Mm -hmm. According to the book that I had on his operations there, I don't know whether you got that or whether I still got it down there. But there's no, there's no flat hay that's being baled. It's other people around. Mostly out on the, on the breaks on the reservation.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, I didn't remember that thing about cattle being fed, ever being fed there. Mm -hmm. This must have been very early in the operation.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: In the 90s. I think it was, I know part of it was when they had the butcher shop. That's something you didn't even know about, I suppose.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Yeah.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I can't think of the other Dutchman's name. He ran off with all of it.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh, yeah, Platt and Bombard.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Bombard, yeah.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Platt and Bombard had a butcher shop once.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I can still remember Grandma one time getting the books out on that butcher shop operation. And you'd be surprised how many people around Genesee who'd Platt and Bombard give a butcher bill that they never did pay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that, that butcher operation was pretty much cutting out the middle man?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, it was, but it went broke anyway.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Probably everybody had their own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Talking about Bombard then, what was the guy's name that drowned down at the Fairwater that was with Eli that time?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It was a name something like that, but I don't know if that was it. The guy that drowned there at the railroad bridge.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Yeah, I know they used to ford that river there. And I remember sitting on Watson's front porch and Eli telling him about that and describing it. But I don't know the name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that happened on this guy, Graham?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, he couldn't swim and they were taking cattle across the river and apparently his horse got in trouble. He didn't get off of him anyway. He was still sitting on him.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Down he went. Apparently the horse just once fell down or went into a deep hole or something there, stepped into a hole, some kind of a hole. It was kind of a dangerous ford anyway.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: This is, as I got the story, this was a heck of a big man anyway. The horse didn't even come up for a while.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I wanted to finish talking about the operation.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: You were sort of the minimum saying about how the two tied together, raising the bulls in the NSE.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there any more about that?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, actually, it was so short -lived because of the intervention of the post -World War, one depression that there wasn't much of a story to tell. It seemed to me a natural putting together of resources. They had the family farm in Tennessee that needed to be used and was a logical place to raise purebred cattle because they needed a different kind of care. Supervised breeding, controlled feeding, used quite a bit of grain and so on that you didn't supply to a range setup. And then, of course, they still had the range setup. They needed a supply of bulls and perhaps occasionally they even weeded out cows from the gurveter and shifted over to that herd to upgrade it. So it made a natural combination of resources. Also, it was the factor that my father and uncle each were fully competent of running either end of this. They didn't beat both of them. They would be the one. So they kind of divided the forces for that. So it was a natural outgrowth of their crimes. Several different standpoints.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a pretty wild country when you were growing up there in the San River?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I mean, I've heard some of the more wild west kind of tales of North Idaho coming out of that country.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, most of that sort of thing is made up, but it wasn't all made up. But it's a rough country. Real estate. You don't go anywhere much there without a saddle horse. It's too hard walking. And there's no other way to fly over it with an airplane, but you can't land one there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there any incidents that come to your mind from the stories that they told in the early days?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, there were rough times. There were sheep men killed and horse thieves hung in one thing and another. Rather than do a man.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Monty Jarrett.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: He had a scar that ran right across there.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: He said a rancher caught him stealing the horse one time. That's where the 30 -30 priest eat. He had to get away with the horse.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, we used to hear allegations of cattle rustling and horse feeding and so on. In our midst, I don't know how much of it was ever confirmed. It's one thing to miss a cow or a steer, another thing to find it in somebody else's possession.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: There was some of that went on.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, I've heard the stories too about that country that somebody said one time about them. A cowboy coming up there from Texas or down in that country. After about a month or so in that salmon river country, he was all through the idea that lands are steep, that the cow couldn't even stay on his side. That wasn't his idea of a place to ride. Heard cows. Find someplace else to herd them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess this happened too, quite regular, didn't it, Tom? Bad winter? Did it slip off the hillsides?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh, you met a cow or a horse will slide there in that country. You go in there now, in some of those places I used to trot and run a saddle horse down. I wouldn't run nothing down that now. You couldn't pay me enough to run a saddle horse down some little hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get snowed in there for months in time in the winter?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: See, they had to come over the hill by forest and come out over the top of the Craig Mountain, so in order to get out of there.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: At that time you could figure on about five feet of snow on Craig Mountain every winter. Unless there was a good deal of travel through, but it kept the trail packed. Well, you could get into a lot of trouble. Well, you could even then, because if it happened to warm up and begin to fall, it would slide off the trail and get bogged down in the snow on the side. And there were years when the head lots more snow than that, because I don't know if you'd remember, Kenneth, but remember where the shop was at the H place on the mountains there, just outside. Well, whenever you remember there were some black pine trees there. Well, there used to be a lot more of those, I guess. But there were three or four stumps out in there. You'd take a horse about cave -wise size and ride out there and just reach over and lay your hand on top of the stumps. H has got caught there one winter and that's where they saw the trees down, on top of the snow or wood. Right there, just about seven feet of snow.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, another index of the answer to your question, Sam, is that people who live down in the bottom of the San River Canyon ordinarily pack in their winter supplies in the fall. And they expect it to be holed up for three to five months before it was practically packed again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember pretty well what you lived on in the way of food during the winter there?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Oh, I suppose I do.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: We usually had plenty of homegrown spuds and quite a lot of canned vegetables. We bought some in cans. I guess mostly what anybody else would live on.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Had your own beef?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: You had bacon and beef. You had hogs too.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: No, we didn't raise hogs. It wasn't very successful. Back in grain, 14 miles, on a pack horse raised hogs. It didn't work either way. You see, in 1919 there was a lot of grain over on Dumac there. Oh, it's in that book. Bill, he bought a whole bunch of the sweets over there, and then bought feeder pigs and took them in there. And along in the wintertime they got ready for market, so we started driving out to Grangeville. We drove them up Whitebird and got over the hill down towards Toll Lake there, and they got them into a barn overnight. And when they went out the next morning, it was zero weather. The damn figures all piled in one corner, and only the ones on top was alive.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: So there wasn't a paying proposition?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: There wasn't a paying proposition.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, my recollection of what we ate included lots of beans and rice and macaroni and almost all kinds of dried fruits. We had relatives in California, and we'd send there and get raisins and dried prunes and wholesale lots.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: And the bacon we bought in whole size, of course.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: And I suppose there was some salt pork and that sort of thing too at the time. Must have had lots of cured and dried stuff that could be packed and kept without a storage problem.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: See, there was no refrigeration in those bags. People that lived where they could cut ice, and of course had ice boxes, but out on the lone ranch, there's not much chance of that. So you had to buy things that could be stored.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the neighbors around you when you were on a ranch there? Did you see other people much, especially the winners?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, occasionally, there was dancers in one thing or another.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It was the winners.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Kind of got them together.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: I suppose by the big standards you'd say you didn't see very much of each other, really.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: How far did Harry live? From us?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Five miles.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Five miles?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: By Saddle Horse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a close neighbor?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, no, we had one neighbor a mile away. As long as Ross was with us, stayed there. But he proved up and moved right out. We had one a mile away in each direction than each two directions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your experience?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Well, I guess from the period, say from about 1910 to 15, when the homesteads were mostly being taken up, and the country was mostly being filled up with young people. We had housewarmings and weddings and that sort of thing. At fairly frequent intervals, it didn't take too much of an excuse to get people to go somewhere.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: No, they kind of looked for an excuse to go somewhere, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But there was no store or was there school in there?

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: It was at our house.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Only private.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
words: Just our family. Our father hired a teacher who lived at our house and taught the family.

speaker: William (Dave) Hickman or Kenneth Platt or Tom Platt
